summary:
Kublai Khan (1215–1294 AD), grandson of [[event:2225|Genghis Khan]] and founder of the Yuan Dynasty, was the most powerful ruler of his age, governing the largest empire in the world from his capital at Dadu (modern Beijing) and completing the [[event:3044|Mongol conquest of China]] with the defeat of the Southern Song Dynasty in 1279. Unlike earlier Mongol conquerors who looted and withdrew, Kublai committed to ruling China as a Chinese-style emperor — adopting Chinese court ceremonies, employing Chinese bureaucrats, and undertaking enormous public works — while maintaining the privileged position of Mongols and Central Asians in the imperial hierarchy.

Kublai Khan's Yuan Dynasty was the vehicle through which [[event:2230|Marco Polo]] and other European travelers experienced the wonders of China, and his court at Dadu was the most cosmopolitan in the world — visited by merchants, diplomats, and scholars from Europe, the Middle East, Central Asia, and Southeast Asia. The dynasty promoted religious tolerance, patronized Buddhist monasteries, and conducted ambitious but ultimately failed military expeditions against Japan (1274 and 1281), Java, Vietnam, and Burma. The [[event:2280|attempted invasions of Japan]] were repelled by typhoons the Japanese called 'kamikaze' (divine winds). The Yuan Dynasty was increasingly resented by the Chinese population, suffering from heavy taxation, inflation caused by over-issued [[event:2217|paper currency]], and natural disasters including floods and plagues. Kublai's successors were increasingly weak, and the dynasty was overthrown by the Chinese nationalist uprising that established the [[event:100|Ming Dynasty]] in 1368.
